content:
# Luke Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 99-100 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_010_page_001.jpg, LUK_010_page_002.jpg

---

**1** After this, the Lord appointed seventy-two others and sent them out ahead of him in pairs to every town and place he was about to visit. **2** He told them, "The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few. Ask the Lord of the harvest, then, to send out workers into his harvest field. **3** Go now! I am sending you out like lambs among wolves. **4** Do not carry a money bag, a traveler's pack, or sandals — and do not stop to greet anyone along the road.

**5** "When you enter a house, first say, 'Peace to this house.' **6** If a person of peace lives there, your peace will rest on them. If not, it will return to you. **7** Stay in that house, eating and drinking whatever they provide, because the worker deserves to be paid. Do not move from house to house.

**8** "When you enter a town and they welcome you, eat what is set before you. **9** Heal the sick who are there and tell them, 'The kingdom of God has come near to you.' **10** But when you enter a town and they do not welcome you, go out into its streets and say, **11** 'Even the dust of your town that clings to our feet, we wipe off against you. But know this: the kingdom of God has come near.' **12** I tell you, on that day it will be more bearable for Sodom than for that town.

**13** "How terrible it will be for you, Chorazin! How terrible for you, Bethsaida! For if the miracles done among you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes. **14** But it will be more bearable for Tyre and Sidon at the judgment than for you. **15** And you, Capernaum — will you be lifted up to heaven? No, you will be thrown down to the realm of the dead.

**16** "Whoever listens to you listens to me. Whoever rejects you rejects me. And whoever rejects me rejects the one who sent me."

**17** The seventy-two returned overflowing with joy. "Lord," they said, "even the demons submit to us when we use your name!"

**18** Jesus replied, "I watched Satan fall from heaven like a flash of lightning. **19** Listen — I have given you authority to trample on snakes and scorpions, and to overcome all the power of the enemy. Nothing will harm you. **20** However, do not rejoice that the spirits submit to you. Rejoice instead that your names are written in heaven."

**21** At that very moment, Jesus was filled with joy through the Holy Spirit and said, "I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and the intellectuals, and revealed them to those as open as little children. Yes, Father, for this was your good pleasure.

**22** "Everything has been entrusted to me by my Father. No one knows who the Son is except the Father, and no one knows who the Father is except the Son — and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him."

**23** Then he turned to his disciples and said privately, "How blessed are the eyes that see what you are seeing! **24** For I tell you, many prophets and kings longed to see what you see but never saw it, and to hear what you hear but never heard it."

**25** On one occasion, a legal expert stood up to test Jesus. "Teacher," he asked, "what must I do to inherit eternal life?"

**26** Jesus said to him, "What is written in the Law? How do you read it?"

**27** He answered, "'Love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your strength, and with all your mind,' and, 'Love your neighbor as yourself.'"

**28** "You have answered correctly," Jesus told him. "Do this, and you will live."

**29** But the man, wanting to justify himself, asked Jesus, "And who is my neighbor?"

**30** In reply, Jesus said: "A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho when he fell into the hands of robbers. They stripped him, beat him, and went away, leaving him half dead. **31** A priest happened to be going down that same road, and when he saw the man, he passed by on the other side. **32** In the same way, a Levite came to the place, saw him, and passed by on the other side. **33** But a Samaritan, as he traveled, came to where the man was. When he saw him, his heart went out to him. **34** He went to him, bandaged his wounds, pouring on oil and wine. Then he put the man on his own donkey, brought him to an inn, and took care of him. **35** The next day, he took out two denarii, gave them to the innkeeper, and said, 'Take care of him. Whatever extra you spend, I will repay you when I come back.'

**36** "Which of these three do you think became a neighbor to the man who fell among the robbers?"

**37** The legal expert replied, "The one who showed him mercy."

Jesus said to him, "Go and do the same."

**38** As Jesus and his disciples traveled on, he entered a certain village where a woman named Martha welcomed him into her home. **39** She had a sister called Mary, who sat at the Lord's feet and listened to what he was saying. **40** But Martha was pulled in every direction by all the preparations she had to make. She came to him and said, "Lord, does it not concern you that my sister has left me to do all the serving alone? Tell her to help me!"

**41** But the Lord answered her, "Martha, Martha, you are worried and upset about so many things, **42** but only one thing is needed. Mary has chosen the better part, and it will not be taken away from her."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **10:1 — "seventy-two others"**: Sinaiticus reads ΕΒΔΟΜΗΚΟΝΤΑ ΔΥΟ (seventy-two), while many later manuscripts read simply "seventy." This is one of the most famous textual variants in Luke. The number seventy echoes the seventy elders of Israel (Exodus 24:1) and the seventy nations of Genesis 10 in the Septuagint. The variant "seventy-two" may reflect an alternative tradition counting seventy-two nations. Either way, the mission to "every town and place" signals a scope far beyond the Twelve — the gospel is meant for all peoples, and the workforce must expand accordingly.

- **10:1 — "sent them out ahead of him in pairs"**: The Greek ΑΝΑ ΔΥΟ (two by two) reflects both practical wisdom and legal principle. In Jewish law, two witnesses were required to establish testimony (Deuteronomy 19:15). The pairs also ensured mutual support and accountability. Jesus does not send his missionaries into hostile territory alone.

- **10:3 — "lambs among wolves"**: The image is deliberately alarming. ΑΡΝΑΣ (arnas, "lambs") are not merely sheep but young, vulnerable lambs — the most defenseless creatures in the pastoral world. Jesus offers no illusion of safety. The mission will succeed not through power or self-defense but through radical vulnerability and divine protection.

- **10:4 — "do not stop to greet anyone along the road"**: This is not rudeness — Near Eastern greetings were elaborate and time-consuming social rituals that could delay a traveler for hours. The instruction communicates extreme urgency: the kingdom message cannot wait for social niceties. Elisha gave Gehazi a similar instruction in 2 Kings 4:29.

- **10:6 — "a person of peace"**: The Semitic expression ΥΙΟΣ ΕΙΡΗΝΗΣ (huios eirenes, literally "son of peace") describes someone whose character and receptivity make them a natural host for the peace of God. The missionaries are not creating peace from nothing — they are discovering where God has already prepared the soil.

- **10:11 — "the kingdom of God has come near"**: The rejected town still hears the message. Even in judgment, the proclamation goes forth. The nearness of the kingdom is an objective reality independent of the town's response — which makes the rejection all the more consequential.

- **10:13 — "Chorazin... Bethsaida"**: These were Jewish towns near Capernaum where Jesus had performed many miracles. Tyre and Sidon were Gentile cities with notorious reputations in the prophetic tradition (Isaiah 23, Ezekiel 26-28). The comparison is devastating: pagan cities with less revelation would have responded better than God's own covenant people. Privilege increases accountability.

- **10:15 — "thrown down to the realm of the dead"**: The Greek ΕΩΣ ΑΔΟΥ (heos hadou) refers to Hades, the underworld — the lowest possible destination. The language echoes Isaiah 14:13-15, the taunt against the king of Babylon who aspired to climb to heaven but was cast to the pit. Capernaum, Jesus' adopted home base, receives the sharpest rebuke of all.

- **10:18 — "I watched Satan fall from heaven like a flash of lightning"**: The verb ΕΘΕΩΡΟΥΝ (etheorown, "I was watching") is imperfect tense — Jesus was observing this in real time, or perhaps from eternity. The seventy-two's successful mission over demons is not merely practical ministry; it enacts a cosmic defeat. The fall of Satan is not future apocalyptic speculation — it is happening now through the advance of the kingdom.

- **10:19 — "snakes and scorpions"**: While literal dangers existed on Palestinian roads, these creatures carry symbolic weight. Snakes and scorpions represent the forces of evil and harm (cf. Psalm 91:13). The authority Jesus grants is ultimately spiritual — power over the entire arsenal of the enemy.

- **10:20 — "your names are written in heaven"**: Jesus redirects the disciples' source of joy from what they can do to who they are. The heavenly registry (cf. Exodus 32:32, Daniel 12:1, Philippians 4:3) represents God's permanent, unshakeable claim on their identity. Ministry success is temporary; belonging to God is eternal.

- **10:21 — "filled with joy through the Holy Spirit"**: This is one of the rare moments where Jesus' inner emotional life is described, and it is one of the most explicitly Trinitarian passages in the Synoptic Gospels: the Son rejoices in the Holy Spirit while praising the Father. The word ΗΓΑΛΛΙΑΣΑΤΟ (egalliastao) denotes deep, exuberant, even ecstatic joy — the same word used of the unborn John leaping in Elizabeth's womb (Luke 1:44).

- **10:21 — "hidden these things from the wise and the intellectuals"**: The Greek ΣΟΦΩΝ ΚΑΙ ΣΥΝΕΤΩΝ (sophon kai syneton) refers to those recognized as learned and discerning by worldly standards. God's revelation operates on a different logic: receptivity matters more than credentials. The ΝΗΠΙΟΙΣ (nepiois, "infants/little children") are not intellectually inferior — they are simply open, unguarded, and willing to receive.

- **10:22 — "No one knows who the Son is except the Father"**: This verse stands out in the Synoptic Gospels as strikingly Johannine in its theology. The mutual, exclusive knowledge between Father and Son implies a relationship of such intimacy that it is impenetrable to outside observation. Knowledge of God is not something humans can achieve — it must be revealed. The Son is both the content of revelation and the one who reveals.

- **10:25 — "a legal expert stood up to test him"**: The word ΝΟΜΙΚΟΣ (nomikos) describes a specialist in the Torah — not a lawyer in the modern sense but a professional interpreter of God's law. His question is not sincere inquiry but a test (ΕΚΠΕΙΡΑΖΩΝ, ekpeirazon), the same root used for Satan's temptation of Jesus in Luke 4. Yet Jesus transforms the hostile encounter into a life-changing teaching moment.

- **10:29 — "wanting to justify himself"**: The phrase ΘΕΛΩΝ ΔΙΚΑΙΩΣΑΙ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ reveals the man's real motivation. He wants to draw a boundary around the word "neighbor" that keeps his obligations manageable. Jesus' parable will explode every such boundary.

- **10:30 — "going down from Jerusalem to Jericho"**: The road descended roughly 3,400 feet over about 17 miles through desolate, rocky terrain known for banditry. Jesus' audience would have recognized this as a notoriously dangerous route. The detail grounds the parable in concrete, believable reality.

- **10:31-32 — "a priest... a Levite... passed by on the other side"**: Both were temple functionaries who might have feared corpse contamination under purity laws (Leviticus 21:1-3, Numbers 19:11-16). Yet they were traveling away from Jerusalem — downhill toward Jericho — so they were not heading to temple duty. Their avoidance cannot be excused by ritual obligation. Jesus exposes the way religious reasoning can become a shield against compassion.

- **10:33 — "But a Samaritan"**: The shock for Jesus' Jewish audience cannot be overstated. Samaritans were despised as religious half-breeds and national enemies. To make the Samaritan the hero was to detonate every assumption about who is capable of neighbor-love. The parable does not merely answer the question "who is my neighbor?" — it redefines the question entirely: stop asking who qualifies as your neighbor, and start becoming a neighbor to whoever is in need.

- **10:34 — "oil and wine"**: These were standard ancient first-aid treatments. Oil soothed wounds; wine served as an antiseptic. The Samaritan applies practical, hands-on care — not pious sentiment from a safe distance. The detail of placing the man on his own donkey means the Samaritan walked, bearing the inconvenience himself.

- **10:35 — "two denarii"**: A denarius was roughly a day's wage for a laborer. Two denarii could cover about two weeks of lodging at a basic inn. The Samaritan's open-ended promise — "whatever extra you spend, I will repay" — represents uncapped generosity. He does not set a budget on compassion.

- **10:37 — "The one who showed him mercy"**: The legal expert cannot bring himself to say the word "Samaritan." He answers with a circumlocution — "the one who showed mercy." Even in conceding the point, his prejudice shows. Jesus' command "Go and do the same" makes the Samaritan the model for a Torah expert's behavior, a complete inversion of the social hierarchy.

- **10:38-39 — "Martha welcomed him... Mary sat at the Lord's feet"**: In first-century Jewish culture, sitting at a rabbi's feet was the posture of a disciple — a role normally reserved for men. Mary is not merely listening casually; she is assuming the position of a student of Torah. Martha's hospitality was culturally expected and genuinely important, but Mary has crossed a social boundary to claim a place in the learning circle.

- **10:41 — "Martha, Martha"**: The doubled name is a Lukan marker of intimate, urgent address (cf. "Simon, Simon" in 22:31, "Saul, Saul" in Acts 9:4). It is tender, not harsh. Jesus sees Martha's genuine distress and speaks to her with deep personal concern.

- **10:42 — "only one thing is needed"**: Sinaiticus reads ΕΝΟΣ ΔΕ ΕΣΤΙΝ ΧΡΕΙΑ — "but one thing is necessary." Some manuscripts expand this to "few things are needed, or only one." The Sinaiticus reading is characteristically spare: in the midst of Martha's many anxieties, Jesus points to the singular essential — attentive presence with him. This is not a rejection of service but a reordering of priorities. Activity for God must flow from time with God, not replace it.
